categories.title: WHAT'S IN A NAME?
categories.theme: Tech companies, products, and protocols known by one name but originally called something else — answer is the ORIGINAL or BETTER-KNOWN NAME

clues.clue: Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne founded this company on April 1st, 1976.
This internet giant began life under the working name 'Cadabra' before pivoting from books to everything.
Microsoft's beloved Windows XP was developed under this single-word codename, borrowed from a ski resort town in British Columbia.
The Tor anonymity network's name is an acronym — these three words are what it originally stood for.
Before it became the dominant dial-up service of the 1990s, America Online ran under this earlier brand name.

categories.title: BITS & BYTES
categories.theme: Fundamental concepts in computer data, memory, and binary representation — answer is the TERM, NUMBER, or UNIT

clues.clue: This is how many bits make up a single nibble — half of a byte.
A single byte can represent exactly this many distinct values, from 0 to 255.
Unlike the marketing kilobyte of 1000 bytes, the International System of Quantities uses this precise term for exactly 1024 bytes.
A single-precision floating-point number reserves exactly this many bits for its significand portion.
On a 32-bit signed integer system, arithmetic cannot go higher than this maximum positive value — roughly 2.1 billion.

finals.title: SECURITY VULNERABILITIES
finals.clue: Discovered in April 2014, this critical flaw in the OpenSSL cryptography library allowed attackers to read protected memory from servers, exposing passwords and private keys worldwide.

finals.title: NETWORKING PIONEERS
finals.clue: Often called the 'Mother of the Internet,' this engineer invented the Spanning Tree Protocol, which prevents network loops in Ethernet bridging.
